turns.content: so something happened at work today and I'm feeling a lot of feelings and I need to talk it out
I'm here for it. Take your time — what happened?

ok so we have this nursing student doing her clinical rotation on our floor, her name is Jasmine and she's 21 and today was her first time doing a real pediatric assessment on her own and she was SO nervous, like shaking nervous. and I was assigned to supervise her
Oh wow, that's a big moment for her. Pediatric assessments are scary even for experienced nurses — kids are unpredictable and the parents are watching your every move. How did she do?

she was amazing honestly. once she got in the room with the kid she just softened up and was so gentle and the kid warmed up to her right away. but the part that got me was afterwards she pulled me aside and said "I almost dropped out of nursing school last semester and watching nurses like you is what made me stay" and I literally almost lost it right there in the hallway

That is incredibly powerful. She saw something in you that made her believe she could do this too. That's not just being a good nurse — that's being the kind of person who changes someone's path just by showing up and doing your job with heart. How are you feeling about it?

honestly I feel like a mess of emotions because this career means more to me than most people realize. nobody in my family had ever gone to college before me, I was the first one. so like every shift I work, every kid I help, it carries all of that weight with it. and hearing that I'm inspiring someone else to keep going?? I can't even explain what that does to me

You don't have to explain it — I can feel it just from how you're writing. Being a first-gen college graduate and then building a career in pediatric nursing is extraordinary. You didn't just open a door for yourself, you became proof for everyone coming after you that it's possible. And now Jasmine is seeing that. That's a legacy right there, Elena.

ok now I'm actually crying thank you lol. it's just, my parents sacrificed SO much for me to get here and sometimes on the hard nights when I'm exhausted and questioning everything I forget why I started. and then something like today happens and it all comes back

Those hard nights are real, and questioning things doesn't mean you're failing — it means you're human and the job is demanding. But moments like today are the universe reminding you that your presence matters beyond just the clinical work. Your parents' sacrifices turned into a daughter who saves kids' lives AND inspires the next generation of nurses. That's not a small thing.

I want to be a good mentor to Jasmine because I didn't really have anyone like that when I was in school, I was kind of figuring it out alone since no one in my family knew how any of it worked. like my parents were so supportive but they couldn't tell me what to expect in clinicals or how to study for the NCLEX you know?

That's exactly what makes you the perfect mentor for her — you know what it's like to navigate all of this without a roadmap. You can give her the guidance you had to figure out on your own. Do you know how long her rotation on your floor lasts? And have you thought about what kind of support you want to offer her beyond just the clinical supervision?

she's on our floor for 6 more weeks. I was thinking of just being available, like giving her my number and telling her she can text me questions anytime. and maybe walking her through some of the stuff I wish someone had told me, like how to handle the emotional part of peds because that's what nobody prepares you for

Both of those are perfect. The emotional side of pediatric nursing is the thing that makes or breaks people, and having someone to process with is everything. Giving her your number sends a huge message — it says "I see you and I've got your back." You might also share some of your own early struggles honestly with her. When mentors are vulnerable about their own journey, it gives students permission to not be perfect.

that's such a good point because I think she looks at me and sees someone who has it together and meanwhile I'm over here running on 4 hours of sleep with anxiety sweats lol. but yeah being honest about the hard parts would probably help her more than pretending everything is fine

Exactly — you don't have to be a superhero to be a great mentor. You just have to be real. Tell her about the nights that are hard, the patients that stay with you, the sleep deprivation, all of it. And tell her you keep going anyway because the work matters. That's way more inspiring than someone who seems to have no struggles at all.

you know what I'm going to talk to her tomorrow night, we're on the same shift. I'm going to tell her she did great today and that I want to be there for her during her rotation. ay dios mio I'm getting emotional again I need to stop lol but this is the stuff that makes the exhaustion worth it

Don't stop — let yourself feel it! This is one of the good days, soak it in. Tell Jasmine exactly what you just told me. And remember this feeling next time you're on a brutal night shift questioning everything. You're exactly where you're supposed to be, doing exactly what you were meant to do.

thank you for letting me ramble about this, I feel like I processed something important tonight. this career, being a nurse, mentoring Jasmine, being the first in my family to do any of this... it all matters and sometimes I just need to hear that. ok I'm going to go wash my face because I look like a raccoon from crying and I have things to do before my shift lol

It absolutely all matters, every bit of it. Go wash your face, get ready for your shift, and tomorrow tell that girl she's got a mentor in her corner. You're doing something really special, Elena. Have a good shift tonight.
